title: Contributing to toolero
text: Function names follow strict verb convention based function primarily : init_() — one-time scaffolding workspace environment. Runs , creates persistent structure, gets way. Example: init_project(). create_() — produces specific file artifact user author edit directly. May called multiple times project. Example: create_qmd(). generate_() — derives assembles output inputs already exist. transformation happening. Example: generate_dockerfile(), generate_kb_xml(). read_() — loads data R session. Nothing written disk. Example: read_clean_csv(). write_() — persists data results session disk. complement read_(). Example: write_by_group().

title: Contributing to toolero
text: R source files use dashes: create-qmd.R, write--group.R R functions objects use underscores: create_qmd(), write_by_group()

title: Contributing to toolero
text: Internal helpers exported follow .function_name() convention leading dot. Example: .validate_file_arg().

title: toolero – Development Journal
text: toolero R package designed help researchers implement best practices coding projects. provides small set opinionated, practical functions reduce friction start project day--day data work. foundational package three-package suite alongside containr (Docker containerization) curriculr (data-driven CV generation). toolero CRAN v0.3.0. developed prior formal journaling practice established curriculr development. journal captures accumulated design decisions evolution package retroactively, continues forward real time v0.4.0.

title: toolero – Development Journal
text: initial release shipped one function: init_project(). job create new R project given path standard folder structure suited research workflows. optionally initializes renv package management git version control. default folder structure: data/, data-raw/, R/, scripts/, plots/, images/, results/, docs/. philosophy behind init_project() one runs toolero: reduce friction starting new project correctly best practices become path least resistance rather extra step.

title: toolero – Development Journal
text: Added uw_branding argument init_project(). TRUE, creates assets/ folder new project populates UW-Madison RCI branding files: styles.css, header.html, rci-banner.png. reflects toolero’s origin internal tool UW-Madison Research Computing Infrastructure workshops consultations.

what: v0.2.0 — Quarto scaffolding and execution context detection
title: toolero – Development Journal
text: Three new functions added. detect_execution_context() identifies three environments code currently running : interactive R session, quarto render call, plain Rscript invocation. Returns one "interactive", "quarto", "rscript". useful writing code resolves input file paths correctly across three contexts – persistent pain point researchers run script interactively, part Quarto document, HPC cluster. create_qmd() scaffolds new Quarto document reproducible template. Ships sample dataset (Palmer Penguins), UW-Madison branded assets, three-context input resolution via detect_execution_context(). Optionally pre-populates YAML header user-supplied YAML config file. write_by_group() splits data frame single grouping column writes group separate CSV file. Filenames derived sanitized group values. Optionally writes manifest.csv. Breaking changes v0.2.0: - create_qmd() path now required, default - write_by_group() output_dir now required, default - init_project() open now defaults FALSE

what: v0.3.0 — KB XML export, purl hook, API refinements
title: toolero – Development Journal
text: generate_kb_xml() produces UW-Madison Knowledge Base importable XML files rendered Quarto documents. Extracts metadata .qmd YAML header (title, description, categories) re-renders embedded resources self-contained import. Addresses specific operational need UW-Madison RCI. create_qmd() purl hook – added use_purl argument (default TRUE) scaffolds _quarto.yml post-render hook purl.R script extracting R code rendered documents R/. purl script uses fs::dir_ls() glob scan rather QUARTO_DOCUMENT_PATH environment variable, proved unreliable across different Quarto invocation methods. Breaking changes v0.3.0: - create_qmd(): filename now first argument default - write_by_group(): sanitized filenames now use dash separators instead underscores - init_project(): file_path argument renamed path

what: What we set out to do
title: toolero – Development Journal
text: Add arborize() toolero – function rendering syntactic trees PNG images using Quarto’s Typst engine. function emerged need migrate linguistics documents LaTeX + tikz/forest Quarto + Typst without losing ability produce standalone tree figures.

title: toolero – Development Journal
text: original workflow used LaTeX packages gb4e/linguex, forest/tikz-qtree, tikz combination. mature carry significant dependency burden. question whether Typst replace workflow standalone tree figure production. Assessment: yes PNG figures embedded documents; LaTeX remains pragmatic full linguistic papers cross-referenced examples.

what: Decision: arborize() as a toolero function
title: toolero – Development Journal
text: function fits toolero better curriculr – toolero general research workflow toolkit rendering standalone figure string exactly kind utility provides.

title: toolero – Development Journal
text: Two Typst packages exist linguistic trees: @preview/syntree:0.2.1 (simple notation) – takes bracket notation string. Compact, familiar linguists, simpler interface. Version 0.2.0 broken Typst 0.12+ due style() function removed. Version 0.2.1 fixes . @preview/lingotree:1.0.0 (structured notation) – takes nested tree() function calls. powerful: supports per-node styling, movement arrows, multi-dominant trees. Released November 2025, compatible current Typst. tree_notation argument controls backend used: - "simple" – bracket notation string, uses syntree 0.2.1 - "structured" – nested tree() calls string, uses lingotree 1.0.0 user provides complete tree string appropriate format. arborize() wraps correct Typst scaffolding.

title: toolero – Development Journal
text: lingotree backend require arborize() parse bracket notation convert nested tree() calls. user responsible providing correct string format. keeps arborize() simple.

title: toolero – Development Journal
text: arborize() performs six steps: 1. Validates inputs resolves Typst package tree_notation 2. Builds minimal .qmd document via .build_arborize_qmd() 3. Writes document inside withr::with_tempdir() automatic cleanup 4. quarto::quarto_render() produces intermediate PDF via Typst 5. pdftools::pdf_convert() converts PDF PNG 6. PNG bytes read memory temp dir deleted, written output path 7. provenance = TRUE, .write_arborize_provenance() writes .yaml file

title: toolero – Development Journal
text: provenance = TRUE (default) writes companion .yaml file alongside PNG recording: tree string, tree_notation, typst_package, dpi, papersize, margin, rendered_by, rendered_at, output path. makes renders reproducible modifiable without hunting original tree string. parameter name provenance chosen save_source, keep_source, write_source meaningful name context – records provenance image, matching DATA-PROVENANCE.md convention established elsewhere toolero. rearborize() function considered rejected v0.4.0. re-render pattern (read yaml, call arborize() recovered arguments) five lines justify exported function. Deferred pending evidence demand.

title: toolero – Development Journal
text: papersize margin arguments determine tightly PNG cropped around tree. key insight: papersize match tree’s complexity, default blindly "a5". Practical guidance: - "a6" "a7" simple trees (2-4 nodes) - "a5" medium trees (default) - "a4" wide deep trees - "a3" wide trees margin provides buffer around tree. Default "0.5cm" works cases. guidance documented arborize() @details vignette.

title: toolero – Development Journal
text: .build_arborize_qmd() returns character string (testable without Quarto). .write_arborize_provenance() writes YAML file (testable without Quarto). arborize() renderer calls produces file. mirrors pattern established curriculr cv_render_section().

title: toolero – Development Journal
text: Simple notation (syntree): tree string goes inside Typst string literal delimited double quotes. Double quotes inside string must escaped. gsub() call: gsub('"', '\\\\"', tree, fixed = TRUE). match escaped result tests: grepl('\\\\"cat\\\\"', result, fixed = TRUE). Four backslashes R pattern = two literal backslashes string. Structured notation (lingotree): tree string goes directly Typst code, inside string literal. escaping needed. User bears syntax responsibility. General principle: user data crosses quoted string literal another language, escaping required. inserted raw code, escaping user bears syntax responsibility.

title: toolero – Development Journal
text: Initial implementation used @preview/syntree:0.2.0 fails current Typst error: unknown variable: style. style() function removed Typst 0.12. Version 0.2.1 (released February 19, 2025) fixes .

title: toolero – Development Journal
text: create_syntree() – original name, wrong verb (create implies persistence) render_syntree() – accurate pipeline-y arborize() – final choice arborize() chosen unexpected, precise, memorable. arborize means form tree-like structure. mild discoverability concern addressed strong roxygen documentation.

title: toolero – Development Journal
text: .build_arborize_qmd() tests: covers backends shared behavior, Quarto required, runs everywhere .write_arborize_provenance() tests: file creation, field contents, filename matching, invisible return – Quarto required arborize() input validation: type errors, empty string, length > 1, NA, existing file, invalid notation, missing pdftools, invalid tree_notation arborize() provenance: provenance = FALSE suppresses yaml, provenance = TRUE writes yaml correct fields arborize() full pipeline: 4 tests (3 simple, 1 structured) – skip_on_ci() skip_on_cran() Path normalization fix pipeline tests: withr::local_tempfile() produces paths // fs::path_abs() normalizes /. Use normalizePath() sides comparing.

title: toolero – Development Journal
text: full vignette written covering: - Motivation: LaTeX tree problem PNG-based solution helps - Two backends: syntree vs lingotree, use - Crop control: papersize margin guidance ISO paper size table - Examples: simple NP, clausal tree, aspectual classes, print-quality, lingotree structured notation, suppressing provenance - Provenance file structure manual re-render pattern - Embedding documents - Argument reference table - References Typst packages vignette uses eval = FALSE globally (arborize() calls never execute build), individual knitr::include_graphics() chunks using eval = TRUE .width display control. PNGs pre-rendered committed vignettes/figures/.

text: vignette took several iterations pass R CMD check due path resolution problem knitr::include_graphics(). root cause single line .Rbuildignore: excluded pre-rendered PNGs package tarball entirely, causing every path approach fail regardless constructed. Approaches tried root cause identified: fig_path() helper, knitr::current_input(dir = TRUE), system.file(“doc”, …), system.file(“vignettes”, …). fix: remove vignettes/figures/ .Rbuildignore. PNGs included tarball, plain relative paths work correctly: Lesson: include_graphics() fails R CMD check, verify files actually tarball debugging paths. pkgbuild::build() tar -tzf toolero_0.4.0.tar.gz | grep figures

title: Rendering Syntactic Trees with arborize()
text: Syntactic trees staple linguistics. Whether analyzing argument structure, working derivation Minimalist framework, simply illustrating constituent structure sentence course handout, need way produce clean, readable tree diagrams. long time, standard answer LaTeX. Packages like forest, tikz-qtree, gb4e served linguists well, remain powerful tools complex diagrams. come significant overhead: full LaTeX installation, custom .sty files, build pipeline can difficult integrate modern R-based workflows. analysis lives Quarto document, Shiny app, R Markdown report, pulling LaTeX just draw tree heavy dependency relatively simple task. situation complicated want embed tree format nothing LaTeX — Word document, Keynote slide, webpage. PDF compiled LaTeX travel well.

title: Rendering Syntactic Trees with arborize()
text: arborize() takes syntactic tree description character string, renders using Quarto’s Typst engine, exports result standalone PNG image. PNG can embedded anywhere: Quarto document, R Markdown report, Word file, presentation, uploaded directly content management system. function handles entire pipeline internally: builds minimal throwaway Quarto document containing tree Quarto renders document via Typst intermediate PDF PDF converted PNG requested resolution Temporary files cleaned automatically Optionally, provenance .yaml file written alongside PNG recording tree string rendering arguments LaTeX required. intermediate files left disk. One function call, one PNG.

title: Rendering Syntactic Trees with arborize()
text: arborize() relies Typst packages hosted Typst package registry. first use, Typst download required package local cache (~/.cache/typst/packages/). requires internet connection. Subsequent renders use cached package work offline.

title: Rendering Syntactic Trees with arborize()
text: arborize() supports two rendering backends, controlled tree_notation argument.

title: Rendering Syntactic Trees with arborize()
text: default backend uses @preview/syntree Typst package, accepts bracket notation. compact input format one linguists already know X-bar theory textbooks LaTeX forest notation. Nodes enclosed square brackets. first element bracket node label; subsequent elements daughter nodes. Leaf nodes appear without brackets. backend suits majority use cases: simple moderately complex phrase structure trees without specialized annotation.
code: [S [NP [Det the] [N cat]] [VP [V sat]]]

title: Rendering Syntactic Trees with arborize()
text: structured backend uses @preview/lingotree Typst package, accepts nested tree() function calls. syntax verbose expressive: supports per-node styling, movement arrows nodes, multi-dominant trees single node appears two parents. backend right choice need fine-grained visual control drawing structures Government Binding Minimalist frameworks movement multi-dominance central. user responsible providing valid tree() syntax. @preview/lingotree documentation authoritative reference full API.
code: tree(   tag: [VP],   tree(     tag: [DP],     [every],     [farmer]   ),   [smiled] )

what: Getting the crop right: papersize and margin
title: Rendering Syntactic Trees with arborize()
text: diving examples, worth understanding papersize margin interact determine final PNG looks like. arborize() renders tree onto Typst page specified papersize. page becomes PDF, converted PNG. page much larger tree, PNG lot empty whitespace around tree — look small lost image. page small, tree clipped. goal choose papersize fits snugly around tree, margin providing comfortable buffer. Think choosing envelope just big enough letter.

previous_headings: Getting the crop right: papersize and margin
title: Rendering Syntactic Trees with arborize()
text: Typst’s paper sizes follow ISO standard series. reference: Start small go tree clipped. simple NP fits "a6". full clausal tree typically needs "a5". aspectual classes tree, branches broadly, benefits "a4".

previous_headings: Getting the crop right: papersize and margin
title: Rendering Syntactic Trees with arborize()
text: margin argument sets equal padding four sides page. default "0.5cm", works well trees. Reduce "0.2cm" tighter crop, increase "1cm" want breathing room around complex tree.

previous_headings: Getting the crop right: papersize and margin
title: Rendering Syntactic Trees with arborize()
text: practical approach sizing new tree: Start papersize = "a5", margin = "0.5cm" (defaults) much whitespace, drop "a6" "a7" tree clipped, move "a4" "a3" Adjust margin final fine-tuning

title: Rendering Syntactic Trees with arborize()
text: basic case — determiner phrase nested noun phrase. simple NP fits well "a6".
code: arborize(   "[NP [Det the] [N cat]]",   output    = "figures/np-tree.png",   papersize = "a6",   margin    = "0.5cm" )

title: Rendering Syntactic Trees with arborize()
text: simple transitive clause subject object. extra branching calls "a5".
code: arborize(   "[S [NP [Det the] [N cat]] [VP [V chased] [NP [Det the] [N dog]]]]",   output    = "figures/clause-tree.png",   papersize = "a5",   margin    = "0.5cm" )

title: Rendering Syntactic Trees with arborize()
text: classic Vendlerian classification verbal aspect — wide, multi-level tree. one needs "a4" accommodate breadth, slightly larger margin gives labels room breathe.
code: arborize(   paste0(     "[Aspectual Classes ",     "[Statives [States]] ",     "[Dynamic ",     "[Atelic [Activities]] ",     "[Telic ",     "[Instantaneous [Achievements]] ",     "[Durative [Accomplishments]]]]]"   ),   output    = "figures/aspectual-classes.png",   papersize = "a4",   margin    = "1cm",   dpi       = 300 )

title: Rendering Syntactic Trees with arborize()
text: figures destined publication, use dpi = 600. default dpi = 300 appropriate screen use general document embedding; 600 minimum print reproduction. Note higher DPI produces larger file change crop — papersize margin still control .
code: arborize(   "[NP [Det the] [N cat]]",   output    = "figures/np-tree-hires.png",   papersize = "a6",   margin    = "0.5cm",   dpi       = 600 )

what: Tight crop with a narrow margin
title: Rendering Syntactic Trees with arborize()
text: trees embedded document padding, tighter crop often looks cleaner. Reducing margin "0.1cm" crops close tree .
code: arborize(   "[NP [Det the] [N cat]]",   output    = "figures/np-tree-tight.png",   papersize = "a6",   margin    = "0.1cm" )

title: Rendering Syntactic Trees with arborize()
text: VP DP internal argument, rendered using lingotree backend. Note tree() string passed verbatim Typst — syntax Typst, R. Always pass tree_notation = "structured" using lingotree; without arborize() defaults syntree backend output incorrect.
code: arborize(   "tree(     tag: [VP],     tree(       tag: [DP],       [every],       [farmer]     ),     [smiled]   )",   tree_notation = "structured",   output        = "figures/vp-lingotree.png",   papersize     = "a6",   margin        = "0.5cm" )

title: Rendering Syntactic Trees with arborize()
text: default arborize() writes companion .yaml file alongside PNG recording tree string rendering arguments. Pass provenance = FALSE suppress .
code: arborize(   "[NP [Det the] [N cat]]",   output     = "figures/np-tree.png",   papersize  = "a6",   provenance = FALSE )

title: Rendering Syntactic Trees with arborize()
text: provenance = TRUE (default), arborize() writes .yaml file stem PNG. call like: directory contain: .yaml file records everything needed reproduce render: re-render provenance file — example increase DPI journal submission keeping exact tree crop settings — read .yaml pass fields back arborize(): provenance file ensures high-resolution version cropped identically original — papersize, margin, different dpi. dedicated rearborize() function wraps pattern yet implemented. useful , please file issue https://github.com/erwinlares/toolero/issues — user demand direct signal feature worth building.
code: arborize(   "[NP [Det the] [N cat]]",   output    = "figures/np-tree.png",   papersize = "a6",   margin    = "0.5cm" ) figures/ ├── np-tree.png └── np-tree.yaml rendered_by: toolero::arborize(), version 0.4.0 rendered_at: 2026-04-30 14:23:11 CDT output: /path/to/figures/np-tree.png tree_notation: simple typst_package: '@preview/syntree:0.2.1' dpi: 300 papersize: a6 margin: 0.5cm tree: '[NP [Det the] [N cat]]' p <- yaml::read_yaml("figures/np-tree.yaml")  arborize(   tree          = p$tree,   output        = "figures/np-tree-hires.png",   dpi           = 600,   tree_notation = p$tree_notation,   papersize     = p$papersize,   margin        = p$margin,   overwrite     = TRUE )

title: Rendering Syntactic Trees with arborize()
text: PNG, embedding R-based document straightforward. Quarto document R Markdown: Quarto document using Markdown syntax directly: arborize() produces standard PNG, tree can also inserted Word documents via officer, added PowerPoint slide via rvg, uploaded directly content management system.
code: ```{{r}} #| label: fig-np-tree #| fig-cap: "A simple NP" #| out-width: "40%" knitr::include_graphics("figures/np-tree.png") ``` ![A simple NP](figures/np-tree.png){width=40%}

text: can install toolero CRAN: install development version GitHub:
code: install.packages("toolero") pak::pak("erwinlares/toolero")

title: Getting started with toolero
text: two functions designed used together, order. init_project() creates scaffold; create_qmd() populates working Quarto document.

title: Getting started with toolero
text: Starting new R project usually means manual steps every time: create folder, set RStudio project, create subdirectories data scripts, initialize renv, initialize git. None steps hard , skipping — especially early — tends create friction later. init_project() handles single call: creates new RStudio project specified path following folder structure already place: structure? folder layout opinionated arbitrary. Separating data/ data-raw/ makes clear files original processed. Keeping R/ distinct scripts/ encourages moving reusable logic functions time, natural step toward maintainable code. default, init_project() also initializes renv git. means project reproducible version-controlled first commit. renv git default? renv ensures packages project depends recorded reproducible. git provides full history changes. much easier set start retrofit later. project needs folders beyond defaults: apply UW-Madison RCI branding assets project: creates assets/ folder populates styles.css, header.html, rci-banner.png — assets used Quarto template scaffolded create_qmd().
code: library(toolero)  init_project(path = "~/Documents/my-project") my-project/ ├── data/         # input data ├── data-raw/     # original, unprocessed data ├── R/            # reusable functions ├── scripts/      # analysis scripts ├── plots/        # generated visualizations ├── images/       # static images and assets ├── results/      # processed outputs and tables └── docs/         # notes, manuscripts, Quarto documents init_project(   path          = "~/Documents/my-project",   extra_folders = c("notebooks", "presentations") ) init_project(   path        = "~/Documents/my-project",   uw_branding = TRUE )

what: Adding a Quarto document with create_qmd()
title: Getting started with toolero

title: Getting started with toolero
text: read_clean_csv() combines readr::read_csv() janitor::clean_names() single call: Column names automatically converted lowercase underscores — consistent, predictable, tidyverse-friendly. column called First Name becomes first_name. Q1 Revenue ($) becomes q1_revenue. default, column type messages readr suppressed. Set verbose = TRUE see :
code: data <- read_clean_csv("data/my-file.csv") data <- read_clean_csv("data/my-file.csv", verbose = TRUE)

what: Splitting data by group with write_by_group()
title: Getting started with toolero
text: data frame contains multiple groups need written separate files, write_by_group() handles split write single call: Output filenames derived group values sanitized use file names — converted lowercase spaces special characters replaced dashes. group called Chinstrap becomes chinstrap.csv. Palmer Penguins become palmer-penguins.csv. also write manifest listing output files, group values, row counts:
code: write_by_group(   data       = penguins,   group_col  = "species",   output_dir = "results/by-species" ) write_by_group(   data       = penguins,   group_col  = "species",   output_dir = "results/by-species",   manifest   = TRUE )

title: Getting started with toolero
text: R code often needs behave differently depending running — interactively RStudio, quarto render, batch Rscript job remote cluster. detect_execution_context() identifies three environments active returns one "interactive", "quarto", "rscript". canonical use case resolving input file paths portably: pattern built template scaffolded create_qmd(), get free without write .
code: context <- detect_execution_context()  input_file <- switch(context,   interactive = "data/sample.csv",   quarto      = params$input_file,   rscript     = commandArgs(trailingOnly = TRUE)[1] )

title: Getting started with toolero
text: section relevant publish content UW-Madison Knowledge Base. , can safely skip . UW-Madison Knowledge Base requires content submitted XML visual assets embedded HTML body. generate_kb_xml() automates process entirely. function: Infers source .qmd HTML path (accepts explicitly via qmd_path) Re-renders document embed-resources: true CSS, images, JavaScript self-contained Extracts metadata .qmd YAML header — title → kb_title, description → kb_summary, categories → kb_keywords Produces .xml file ready direct KB import description categories fields create_qmd() template matter — flow automatically KB article metadata without extra work. importing KB, check Decode HTML entity body content option.

what: The problem with starting from scratch
title: A Toolkit for Research Workflows
text: Every research coding project begins blank slate set early decisions: put data, name scripts, whether track dependencies, whether use version control. decisions feel low-stakes moment. rarely . cost usually appears later, project needs shared, reviewed, rerun, containerized, moved larger computing system. project starts flat folder, dependency tracking, scripts mix data loading, cleaning, modeling, reporting impossible rescue later — genuinely hard. Collaborators reproduce results package versions unknown. analysis breaks moved different machine. manuscript references outputs longer exist file system. toolero small, opinionated set tools designed make good research workflow decisions easier adopt. impose rigid framework. provides practical defaults common research projects gets way need customize. new research computing, toolero gives solid starting point without requiring know advance piece matters. experienced, automates setup work otherwise hand start every project.

title: A Toolkit for Research Workflows
text: Use toolero : starting new research coding project; teaching students collaborators reproducible project structure; preparing analysis may later need run outside laptop; using Quarto source truth analysis; splitting data independent pieces parallel high-throughput workflows; standardizing setup across multiple projects; publishing technical documentation stay synchronized source. toolero useful . need containerize project submit work cluster benefit better project structure, cleaner inputs, literate analysis documents, repeatable workflows.

title: A Toolkit for Research Workflows
text: toolero also first step three-package family reproducible research workflows, local project setup containerization high-throughput computing submission: package useful . Together, form path new local R project containerized analysis can run high-throughput computing infrastructure. can adopt packages one time. toolero require containr, containr require submitr. family exists step prepares cleanly next project ready scale.

title: A Toolkit for Research Workflows
text: Install CRAN: Install development version GitHub:
code: install.packages("toolero") # install.packages("pak") pak::pak("erwinlares/toolero")

title: A Toolkit for Research Workflows
text: functions cover common path project creation analysis-ready data. example uses temporary directory can try workflow without writing Documents folder. real project, replace project_dir path want project live. important idea toolero helps start structure can grow: local analysis first, reproducible execution later, scalable computing needed.
code: library(toolero)  project_dir <- file.path(tempdir(), "my-analysis")  # 1. Create a project with sensible defaults init_project(path = project_dir)  # 2. Scaffold a reproducible Quarto analysis document create_qmd(path = project_dir, filename = "analysis.qmd")  # 3. Read and clean a CSV file input_file <- file.path(project_dir, "data-raw", "input.csv") data <- read_clean_csv(input_file)  # 4. Split data into per-group subsets for parallel processing write_by_group(   data,   group_col  = "species",   output_dir = file.path(project_dir, "data", "jobs"),   manifest   = TRUE )

title: A Toolkit for Research Workflows
text: Creates new R project standard folder structure suited research workflows. Optionally initializes renv dependency management git version control — default, matter. default structure includes data/, data-raw/, R/, scripts/, plots/, images/, results/, docs/. Extra folders can added without disrupting defaults. renv lockfile init_project() creates also containr::generate_dockerfile() reads containerize project later. Starting init_project() means step already prepared, even never need .
code: # Standard project init_project(path = "~/Documents/my-project")  # With additional folders init_project(   path = "~/Documents/my-project",   extra_folders = c("notebooks", "presentations") )

title: A Toolkit for Research Workflows
text: Scaffolds new Quarto document reproducible template, including sample dataset, UW-Madison branded assets, optional YAML pre-population, optional post-render hook automatically extracts R code rendered document companion .R file. function two main motivations. First, reduces repetitive setup work. regularly create Quarto documents author information, institutional metadata, preferred format settings, project conventions, yaml_data argument lets pre-populate YAML header personal configuration file instead rebuilding header hand. Second, helps reduce code drift. literate programming workflow, .qmd document can serve source truth: prose, code, results, interpretation live together. post-render hook derives standalone .R script document automatically, maintain separate script hand. pattern discussed detail post Notebook Cluster. Part 1: Start Document. companion .R script can become script run Rscript, containerize containr, send CHTC submitr, confirm runs cleanly outside interactive session. Writing analysis Quarto document deriving executable script automatically helps prevent report runnable analysis drifting apart.
code: # Create an analysis document with the purl hook (default) create_qmd(path = "~/Documents/my-project", filename = "analysis.qmd")  # Without the purl hook create_qmd(   path = "~/Documents/my-project",   filename = "report.qmd",   use_purl = FALSE )  # Pre-populate YAML from a personal config file create_qmd(   path = "~/Documents/my-project",   filename = "analysis.qmd",   yaml_data = "~/my_config.yml" )

title: A Toolkit for Research Workflows
text: Reads CSV file tibble cleans column names one step. Column names become lowercase, spaces become underscores, special characters removed. small thing, consistently start prevents subtle downstream errors. read_clean_csv() built common data-ingest tools toolero dependency stack, including readr, janitor, tibble.
code: data <- read_clean_csv("data-raw/input.csv")  # Show column type messages data <- read_clean_csv("data-raw/input.csv", verbose = TRUE)

title: A Toolkit for Research Workflows
text: Many reproducibility problems come code works place written. detect_execution_context() helps one analysis behave correctly whether run interactively, rendered Quarto, called Rscript. function identifies three environments code currently running — interactive R session, quarto render call, plain Rscript invocation — returns "interactive", "quarto", "rscript". matters code drift often begins tiny differences script launched. write one input path working interactively, another rendering document, third preparing command-line version scheduler. time, entry points drift apart. path updated one place another. parameter handled differently. local version works, rendered scheduled version . Using detect_execution_context() gives one place decide inputs resolved: pattern keeps source logic portable across RStudio, Quarto, command-line execution. Quarto document later converted standalone .R script, context-aware input logic comes along . important part: document script need separate versions data-loading code.
code: context <- detect_execution_context()  input_file <- switch(context,   interactive = "data/sample.csv",   quarto      = params$input_file,   rscript     = commandArgs(trailingOnly = TRUE)[1] )

title: A Toolkit for Research Workflows
text: Splits data frame grouping column writes group separate CSV file. Filenames derived sanitized group values — lowercase, spaces special characters replaced dashes. Optionally writes manifest.csv listing output files, group values, row counts. useful time project needs independent input files: one file per county, participant, simulation parameter, model specification, bootstrap replicate, image tile, study site. manifest becomes map work done. high-throughput workflows, manifest input submitr::htc_gen_submit() multiple-job mode. Splitting data write_by_group(manifest = TRUE) produces structure needed submit one HTCondor job per group.
code: sample_path <- system.file("templates", "sample.csv", package = "toolero") penguins    <- read_clean_csv(sample_path)  # Split by species and write a manifest write_by_group(   penguins,   group_col  = "species",   output_dir = "data/jobs",   manifest   = TRUE )

title: A Toolkit for Research Workflows
text: Renders syntactic tree standalone PNG image using Quarto’s Typst engine. Accepts bracket notation simple trees structured notation trees requiring movement arrows per-node styling. function comes practical problem linguistics: creating good-looking syntactic trees often work . arborize() gives linguists reproducible way generate clean tree images text-based notation. tree specification can live script, vignette, Quarto document, teaching repository, image can regenerated later instead manually redrawn. Requires Quarto 1.4+ Typst support pdftools package.
code: # Simple bracket notation arborize(   "[NP [Det the] [N cat]]",   output    = "figures/np-tree.png",   papersize = "a6" )

what: Render a syntactic tree as a PNG image — arborize
title: Render a syntactic tree as a PNG image — arborize
text: Takes syntactic tree renders using Quarto's Typst engine, exporting result PNG image. Supports two rendering backends controlled tree_notation:

title: Render a syntactic tree as a PNG image — arborize
code: arborize(   tree,   output = "syntactic-tree.png",   dpi = 300,   tree_notation = c("simple", "structured"),   papersize = "a5",   margin = "0.5cm",   provenance = TRUE,   overwrite = FALSE )

title: Render a syntactic tree as a PNG image — arborize
text: tree character string. tree_notation = "simple", bracket notation string e.g. "[S [NP] [VP]]". tree_notation = "structured", lingotree tree() call string. output character string. Path output PNG file. Defaults "syntactic-tree.png" current working directory. dpi numeric value. Resolution output PNG dots per inch. Defaults 300. Use 600 print-quality output. tree_notation character string. One "simple" (default) "structured". Controls Typst rendering backend used. See Details. papersize character string. Typst paper size intermediate PDF. Defaults "a5". Increase "a4" wide trees. margin character string. Page margin intermediate PDF. Defaults "0.5cm". Reduce tighter crops around tree. provenance logical. Whether write companion .yaml file recording tree string rendering arguments alongside PNG. Defaults TRUE. provenance file name PNG .yaml extension lives directory. Pass FALSE suppress . overwrite logical. Whether overwrite existing output files. TRUE, overwrites PNG provenance file exist. Defaults FALSE.

title: Render a syntactic tree as a PNG image — arborize
text: Invisibly returns path output PNG file.

title: Render a syntactic tree as a PNG image — arborize
text: "simple" uses @preview/syntree accepts bracket notation string, e.g. "[S [NP [Det ] [N cat]] [VP [V sat]]]". compact input format suits basic linguistic trees. "structured" uses @preview/lingotree accepts nested tree() call string. backend supports per-node styling, movement arrows, multi-dominant trees. function useful producing standalone tree figures can embedded document format – LaTeX, Word, HTML, presentations – without requiring full LaTeX installation. arborize() performs following steps: Validates inputs resolves Typst package tree_notation. Builds minimal .qmd document via .build_arborize_qmd(). Writes document renders inside self-cleaning temporary directory managed withr::with_tempdir(). Calls quarto::quarto_render() produce intermediate PDF via Typst. Converts PDF PNG using pdftools::pdf_convert(). Reads PNG bytes memory temporary directory deleted, writes specified output path. provenance = TRUE, writes companion .yaml file recording tree string rendering arguments. first use, Typst download required package Typst package registry. requires internet connection. Subsequent renders use locally cached package. Requires Quarto 1.4 later Typst support, pdftools package PDF--PNG conversion. Install pdftools install.packages("pdftools").

title: Render a syntactic tree as a PNG image — arborize
text: syntree Typst package (v0.2.1): https://typst.app/universe/package/syntree lingotree Typst package (v1.0.0): https://typst.app/universe/package/lingotree

title: Render a syntactic tree as a PNG image — arborize
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Simple bracket notation (default) -- also writes tree-1.yaml arborize("[NP [Det the] [N cat]]", output = "my-trees/tree-1.png")  # Suppress provenance file arborize("[NP [Det the] [N cat]]", provenance = FALSE)  # Wider tree with print-quality output arborize(   paste0(     "[Aspectual Classes ",     "[Statives [States]] ",     "[Dynamic ",     "[Atelic [Activities]] ",     "[Telic ",     "[Instantaneous [Achievements]] ",     "[Durative [Accomplishments]]]]]"   ),   output    = "aspectual-classes.png",   dpi       = 600,   papersize = "a4" )  # Structured notation using lingotree arborize(   "tree(     tag: [VP],     tree(       tag: [DP],       [every],       [farmer]     ),     [smiled]   )",   tree_notation = "structured",   output        = "vp-tree.png" ) } # }

what: Create a new Quarto document from a template — create_qmd
title: Create a new Quarto document from a template — create_qmd
text: Creates new Quarto document specified directory, along sample dataset UW-Madison branded assets. Optionally pre-populates YAML header user-supplied metadata.

title: Create a new Quarto document from a template — create_qmd
code: create_qmd(   filename = NULL,   path = ".",   yaml_data = NULL,   overwrite = FALSE,   use_purl = TRUE )

title: Create a new Quarto document from a template — create_qmd
text: filename string NULL. Name generated .qmd file. Must supplied explicitly, e.g. "analysis.qmd". path string. Path directory document created. Defaults "." (current working directory). yaml_data string NULL. Path YAML file containing metadata pre-populate document header. NULL (default), template copied -placeholder prompts intact. overwrite logical. Whether overwrite existing files. Defaults FALSE. use_purl Logical. TRUE (default), creates _quarto.yml file post-render hook purl.R script inside R/ extracts R code rendered document .R file. target document resolved dynamically scanning project root .qmd files, purl.R works regardless document name.

title: Create a new Quarto document from a template — create_qmd
text: Invisibly returns path.

title: Create a new Quarto document from a template — create_qmd
text: create_qmd() performs following steps: Validates path exists. Validates filename supplied. Creates data/ folder path copies sample.csv . Checks assets/styles.css assets/header.html - creates assets/ folder needed copies package. Copies template .qmd path/filename. yaml_data provided, reads YAML file substitutes values document header. use_purl = TRUE, writes _quarto.yml post-render hook pointing R/purl.R, copies purl.R package templates path/R/purl.R. sample dataset bundled template subset Palmer Penguins dataset. Citation: Horst , Hill AP, Gorman KB (2020). palmerpenguins: Palmer Archipelago (Antarctica) Penguin Data. R package version 0.1.0. doi:10.5281/zenodo.3960218 Note: filename default value must always supplied explicitly. Use tempdir() temporary output testing exploration.

title: Create a new Quarto document from a template — create_qmd
code: # \donttest{ # Create a document in a temp directory create_qmd(path = tempdir(), filename = "analysis.qmd") #> ✔ Created /tmp/Rtmpip9DJO/data/sample.csv #> ✔ Created /tmp/Rtmpip9DJO/assets/styles.css #> ✔ Created /tmp/Rtmpip9DJO/assets/header.html #> ✔ Created /tmp/Rtmpip9DJO/analysis.qmd #> ✔ Created /tmp/Rtmpip9DJO/_quarto.yml #> ✔ Created /tmp/Rtmpip9DJO/R/purl.R  # Create with a custom filename, without the purl hook create_qmd(path = tempdir(), filename = "report.qmd",             overwrite = TRUE, use_purl = FALSE) #> ✔ Created /tmp/Rtmpip9DJO/data/sample.csv #> ✔ Created /tmp/Rtmpip9DJO/assets/styles.css #> ✔ Created /tmp/Rtmpip9DJO/assets/header.html #> ✔ Created /tmp/Rtmpip9DJO/report.qmd  # Create with pre-populated YAML yaml_file <- tempfile(fileext = ".yml") writeLines("author:\n  - name: 'Your Name'", yaml_file) create_qmd(path = tempdir(), filename = "analysis.qmd",             yaml_data = yaml_file, overwrite = TRUE) #> ✔ Created /tmp/Rtmpip9DJO/data/sample.csv #> ✔ Created /tmp/Rtmpip9DJO/assets/styles.css #> ✔ Created /tmp/Rtmpip9DJO/assets/header.html #> ✔ Created /tmp/Rtmpip9DJO/analysis.qmd #> ✔ Created /tmp/Rtmpip9DJO/_quarto.yml #> ✔ Created /tmp/Rtmpip9DJO/R/purl.R # }

what: Detect the current execution context — detect_execution_context
title: Detect the current execution context — detect_execution_context
text: Identifies three execution environments code currently running : interactive R session, quarto render call, plain Rscript invocation. useful writing code behaves correctly across three contexts, resolving input file paths portable way.

title: Detect the current execution context — detect_execution_context
code: detect_execution_context(interactive_fn = interactive)

title: Detect the current execution context — detect_execution_context
text: interactive_fn function. Used detect whether session interactive. Defaults base::interactive. Override tests simulate different execution environments.

title: Detect the current execution context — detect_execution_context
text: character string, one "interactive", "quarto", "rscript".

title: Detect the current execution context — detect_execution_context
text: Detection follows priority order: interactive() TRUE, returns "interactive". environment variable QUARTO_DOCUMENT_PATH set non-empty, returns "quarto". Otherwise, returns "rscript".

title: Detect the current execution context — detect_execution_context
code: # \donttest{ context <- detect_execution_context()  input_file <- switch(context,   interactive = "data/sample.csv",   quarto      = params$input_file,   rscript     = commandArgs(trailingOnly = TRUE)[1] ) # }

title: Build a throwaway Quarto document for syntactic tree rendering — .build_arborize_qmd
text: tree character string. syntactic tree notation appropriate tree_notation. tree_notation character string. One "simple" "structured". typst_package character string. resolved Typst package import string, derived internally tree_notation. papersize character string. Typst paper size. margin character string. Page margin.

title: Build a throwaway Quarto document for syntactic tree rendering — .build_arborize_qmd
text: character string containing complete .qmd file content.

title: Build a throwaway Quarto document for syntactic tree rendering — .build_arborize_qmd
text: "simple" uses @preview/syntree bracket notation "structured" uses @preview/lingotree nested tree() calls Separating builder arborize() makes QMD content testable without requiring Quarto installation.

text: output character string. Absolute path PNG output file. tree character string. tree string passed arborize(). tree_notation character string. One "simple" "structured". typst_package character string. resolved Typst package. dpi numeric value. DPI used rendering. papersize character string. Typst paper size used. margin character string. Page margin used.

what: Generate a KB-importable XML file from a Quarto document — generate_kb_xml
title: Generate a KB-importable XML file from a Quarto document — generate_kb_xml
text: Takes Quarto document produces XML file directly importable UW-Madison Knowledge Base (KB) article. function re-renders .qmd embed-resources: true visual assets self-contained, extracts HTML body, wraps KB XML structure along metadata drawn document's YAML header.

title: Generate a KB-importable XML file from a Quarto document — generate_kb_xml
code: generate_kb_xml(html_path, qmd_path = NULL, output_dir = NULL)

title: Generate a KB-importable XML file from a Quarto document — generate_kb_xml
text: html_path string. Path rendered HTML file. Used infer output filename , qmd_path NULL, location source .qmd. qmd_path string NULL. Path source .qmd file. NULL (default), inferred replacing .html extension html_path .qmd. output_dir string NULL. Directory .xml file written. NULL (default), written directory html_path.

title: Generate a KB-importable XML file from a Quarto document — generate_kb_xml
text: Invisibly returns path written .xml file.

title: Generate a KB-importable XML file from a Quarto document — generate_kb_xml
text: generate_kb_xml() performs following steps: Validates html_path exists. Infers qmd_path html_path supplied, validates . Extracts title, description, categories .qmd YAML header maps kb_title, kb_summary, kb_keywords. Re-renders .qmd isolated temporary directory embed-resources: true CSS, images, JS self-contained. data/ assets/ folders copied alongside .qmd ensure render succeeds. Extracts <body> embedded HTML. Escapes HTML entities body XML compatibility, required UW-Madison KB import format. Builds XML structure kb_title, kb_keywords, kb_summary, kb_body nodes. Writes .xml file output_dir. Temporary files managed via withr::local_tempdir() automatically cleaned function exits, even error. importing resulting XML KB, check Decode HTML entity body content option.

title: Generate a KB-importable XML file from a Quarto document — generate_kb_xml

what: Initialize a new R project with a standard folder structure — init_project
title: Initialize a new R project with a standard folder structure — init_project
text: init_project() creates new R project given path opinionated folder structure suited research workflows. optionally initializes renv package management git version control.

title: Initialize a new R project with a standard folder structure — init_project
code: init_project(   path,   use_renv = TRUE,   use_git = TRUE,   extra_folders = NULL,   open = FALSE,   uw_branding = FALSE )

title: Initialize a new R project with a standard folder structure — init_project
text: path character string path name new project (e.g., "~/Documents/-project"). use_renv Logical. TRUE, initializes renv new project. Defaults TRUE. use_git Logical. TRUE, initializes git repository new project. Defaults TRUE. extra_folders character vector additional folder names create inside project. Defaults NULL. open Logical. TRUE, opens new project RStudio creation. Defaults TRUE. uw_branding Logical. TRUE, creates assets/ folder populates UW-Madison RCI branding files (styles.css, header.html, rci-banner.png). Defaults FALSE.

title: Initialize a new R project with a standard folder structure — init_project
text: Called side effects. return value.

title: Initialize a new R project with a standard folder structure — init_project
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ init_project(path = file.path(tempdir(), "project1"),              use_renv = FALSE, use_git = FALSE)  init_project(path = file.path(tempdir(), "project2"),              uw_branding = TRUE, use_renv = FALSE, use_git = FALSE)  init_project(path = file.path(tempdir(), "project3"),              extra_folders = c("notebooks"),              use_renv = FALSE, use_git = FALSE) } # }

what: Read and clean a CSV file — read_clean_csv
title: Read and clean a CSV file — read_clean_csv
text: read_clean_csv() reads CSV file cleans column names one step. leverages readr::read_csv() reading janitor::clean_names() making column names tidyverse-friendly (lowercase, spaces, special characters). default, column type messages suppressed. Set verbose = TRUE display .

title: Read and clean a CSV file — read_clean_csv
code: read_clean_csv(file_path, verbose = FALSE)

title: Read and clean a CSV file — read_clean_csv
text: file_path character string path CSV file. verbose Logical. TRUE, displays column type messages readr::read_csv(). Defaults FALSE.

title: Read and clean a CSV file — read_clean_csv
text: tibble clean column names.

title: Read and clean a CSV file — read_clean_csv
code: # \donttest{ # Read and clean a CSV file silently sample_path <- system.file("templates", "sample.csv", package = "toolero") data <- read_clean_csv(sample_path)  # Show column type messages data <- read_clean_csv(sample_path, verbose = TRUE) #> Rows: 344 Columns: 8 #> ── Column specification ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Delimiter: "," #> chr (3): species, island, sex #> dbl (5): bill_length_mm, bill_depth_mm, flipper_length_mm, body_mass_g, year #>  #> ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data. #> ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message. # }

what: toolero: A Toolkit for Research Workflows — toolero-package
title: toolero: A Toolkit for Research Workflows — toolero-package
text: Provides utility functions help researchers implement best practices coding projects. Includes tools reading cleaning data files, initializing R projects standard folder structure, creating Quarto documents reproducible template, detecting execution context across interactive, Quarto, script-based workflows, splitting data frames group-level output files.

title: toolero: A Toolkit for Research Workflows — toolero-package
text: Maintainer: Erwin Lares erwin.lares@wisc.edu (ORCID) Authors: Erwin Lares erwin.lares@wisc.edu (ORCID)

what: Split a data frame by a grouping column and write each group to a CSV file — write_by_group
title: Split a data frame by a grouping column and write each group to a CSV file — write_by_group
text: Splits data frame single grouping column writes group separate CSV file. Optionally writes manifest file listing output files, group values, row counts.

title: Split a data frame by a grouping column and write each group to a CSV file — write_by_group
code: write_by_group(data, group_col, output_dir = NULL, manifest = FALSE)

title: Split a data frame by a grouping column and write each group to a CSV file — write_by_group
text: data data frame tibble split save. group_col string. name column group . output_dir string NULL. Path directory output files written. Created exist. NULL, user must supply path explicitly. manifest logical. Whether write manifest.csv file output_dir listing output files, group values, row counts. Defaults FALSE.

title: Split a data frame by a grouping column and write each group to a CSV file — write_by_group
text: Invisibly returns output_dir.

title: Split a data frame by a grouping column and write each group to a CSV file — write_by_group
text: Output filenames derived group values group_col. Values sanitized use filenames: converted lowercase, spaces special characters replaced -, consecutive dashes collapsed, leading/trailing dashes stripped. manifest = TRUE, manifest.csv written output_dir containing three columns: group_value, n_rows, file_path. Note: output_dir default value. Always supply explicit path avoid writing files unexpected locations. Use tempdir() temporary output testing exploration.

title: Split a data frame by a grouping column and write each group to a CSV file — write_by_group
code: # \donttest{ # Split a small data frame by group and write to a temp directory data <- data.frame(   species = c("Adelie", "Adelie", "Gentoo"),   mass    = c(3750, 3800, 5000) ) write_by_group(data, group_col = "species", output_dir = tempdir()) #> ✔ Written "Adelie" (2 rows) to /tmp/Rtmpip9DJO/adelie.csv #> ✔ Written "Gentoo" (1 rows) to /tmp/Rtmpip9DJO/gentoo.csv  # Same but also write a manifest write_by_group(data, group_col = "species",                output_dir = tempdir(), manifest = TRUE) #> ✔ Written "Adelie" (2 rows) to /tmp/Rtmpip9DJO/adelie.csv #> ✔ Written "Gentoo" (1 rows) to /tmp/Rtmpip9DJO/gentoo.csv #> ✔ Manifest written to /tmp/Rtmpip9DJO/manifest.csv # }

title: toolero 0.4.0
text: "simple" (default) – bracket notation string, uses @preview/syntree:0.2.1 "structured" – nested tree() call string, uses @preview/lingotree:1.0.0 Output PNG files can embedded document format without requiring LaTeX installation. Requires Quarto 1.4+ Typst support pdftools package (Suggests).

title: toolero 0.3.0
text: CRAN release: 2026-04-27

title: toolero 0.3.0
text: create_qmd(): filename now first argument default – must supplied explicitly. path now second argument defaults ".", allowing natural calls like create_qmd("analysis.qmd"). write_by_group(): sanitized output filenames now use - (dash) separator instead _ (underscore), consistent package convention file names use dashes. Existing workflows reference output paths name need update accordingly. init_project(): file_path argument renamed path consistency create_qmd() broader package API. Calls using file_path = name error; positional calls unaffected.

title: toolero 0.3.0
text: Added generate_kb_xml() produce UW-Madison KB-importable XML files rendered Quarto documents. Extracts metadata .qmd YAML header re-renders embedded resources self-contained import. create_qmd(): added use_purl argument (default TRUE) scaffolds _quarto.yml post-render hook purl.R script extracting R code rendered documents R/.

title: toolero 0.3.0
text: init_project(): now runs renv::snapshot() creates .renvignore renv::init(), ensuring lockfile populated .qmd files excluded dependency scanning project creation time. create_qmd(): _quarto.yml now copied inst/templates/ rather written hardcoded string, changes template reflected automatically. create_qmd(): purl.R now correctly placed R/ instead project root, consistent _quarto.yml calling Rscript R/purl.R. create_qmd(): fixed YAML boolean serialization yaml_data supplied. yaml::.yaml() converting true/false yes/, Quarto recognize. custom handler now forces unquoted true/false output. inst/templates/purl.R: replaced QUARTO_DOCUMENT_PATH environment variable approach fs::dir_ls() glob scan, works reliably regardless Quarto invokes post-render script.

title: toolero 0.2.0
text: CRAN release: 2026-04-24

title: toolero 0.2.0
text: create_qmd(): path now required argument default. Passing NULL omitting raises error. Use tempdir() temporary output. write_by_group(): output_dir now required argument default. Passing NULL omitting raises error. Use tempdir() temporary output. init_project(): open now defaults FALSE instead TRUE avoid disrupting current RStudio session non-interactive contexts.

title: toolero 0.2.0
text: Added detect_execution_context() identify whether code running interactive R session, quarto render call, plain Rscript invocation. Returns one "interactive", "quarto", "rscript". Added create_qmd() scaffold new Quarto document reproducible template, including sample dataset, UW-Madison branded assets, three-context input resolution via detect_execution_context(). Optionally pre-populates YAML header user-supplied YAML config file. Added write_by_group() split data frame single grouping column write group separate CSV file. Filenames derived sanitized group values. Optionally writes manifest.csv listing output files, group values, row counts.

title: toolero 0.1.1
text: Added uw_branding argument init_project(). TRUE, creates assets/ folder new project populates UW-Madison RCI branding files (styles.css, header.html, rci-banner.png).

title: toolero 0.1.0
text: Initial CRAN submission.
